coredata.dc:description: The development of environment-friendly and efficient biological antibacterial dressings is of great significance for the repair and treatment of infectious wounds. Here, we constructed poly (vinyl alcohol)/chitosan-based films and combined them with the photoresponse properties of fluorescent carbon quantum dots extracted from natural ginger plants to develop a series of photodynamic infection wound dressings with high antibacterial properties (PCH-CDs). The composite film adapted to the patient's wound motion, had reliable mechanical properties. The results showed that the incorporation of carbon quantum dots enabled PCH-CDs2 to induce ROS production efficiently under light irradiation, and >2.20 Log10 CFU/mL of Staphylococcus aureus was inactivated after irradiation with a 405 nm light source for 40 min. The composite film showed excellent bacteriostatic and healing effects in a mouse model of infected wounds. Therefore, composite films provide efficient photodynamic antimicrobial therapy, which are promising candidate materials for the clinical application of infected wound dressings in the future.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the preparation and properties of composite hydrogel films loaded with carbon quantum dots (PCH-CDs). (A) Schematic diagram of composite film synthesis. (B) schematic diagram of the application of the film in vivo.
(a) SEM micrographs of surfaces (top) and cross-sections (bottom) of the films. (b) Fourier transform infrared spectra.

(a) Digital picture of the film, (b) UV–visible transmittance spectrum of the film (Insert picture: ultraviolet color indicator card picture covered with CS film and PCH-CDs2 film after ultraviolet light irradiation), (c) thickness of films, (d) tensile strength and elongation at break, (e) the film can bend and fold and adhere to the surface of the skin.
(a) Swelling ratio, (b) water vapor permeability, (c) water contact angle.

(a) Percentage cell viability of L929 cells after 24 h on composite films measured by CCK8 kit. Cell migration (b) and quantification curves (c) of L929 cells in different treatment groups within 60 h. Scale bar: 100 μm. (d) Pictures of blood clot formation over time.

Generation of singlet oxygen: (a) Changes in the absorption peaks of DPBF in pure water at different irradiation time (405 nm, 20 mW/cm2). Changes in the absorption peaks of (b) DPBF + turmeric carbon quantum dots (CDs) and (c) DPBF + composite hydrogel films loaded with 0.5 wt% concentrations of CDs (PCH-CDs2) at different irradiation time. (d) The relative decreasing trend of DPBF absorbance after different sample treatment.

In vitro antibacterial properties: (a) PDT schematic diagram of the composite film. (b) Images of plate surviving colonies of S. aureus and E. coli after treatment with composite film and irradiation for 30 min (405 nm,20 mW/cm2). Number of surviving colonies of (c) S. aureus and (d) E. coli after treatment with composite film at different irradiation time counted by plate counting method. (e) Images of stained live and dead bacteria after treatment with composite film and light (30 min irradiation time, 405 nm, 20 mW/cm2), where blue fluorescence indicates live bacteria and red fluorescence indicates dead bacteria. Scale bar: 25 μm.

Treatment efficacy of infected wounds in mice, where PCH3 group is composite hydrogel films with PVA, CS w/t and HA solutions mixed in a ratio of 6:3:1, while the PCH-CDs2 group is composite hydrogel films loaded with 0.5 wt% concentration of CDs: (a) Flow chart of the modeling and treatment of infective wound model in mice. (b) Representative photos of skin wounds at different time points. (c) Schematic diagram of wound residual area in mice. (d) Photographs of bacterial plate cultures obtained from wound tissue and (f) counts of colony numbers. (e) Quantitative analysis of wound area. (g) Weight changes in the mice. All data were expressed as mean ± SD, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.01.

Histopathological analysis: (a) Hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining. (b) Masson staining. (c) Photographs of CD31 staining. (d) Photographs of TNF-α staining. Scale bar: 40 μm. Statistics on the relative expression of (e) Fibrous tissue, (f) CD31 and (g) TNF-α. (h) H&E staining of major organs after different treatments. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01. Scale bar: 100 μm.

head.ce:title: Natural fluorescent carbon quantum dots embedded polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan film with photoregulation and high antibacterial efficiency for infected wound healing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of environment-friendly and efficient biological antibacterial dressings is of great significance for the repair and treatment of infectious wounds. Here, we constructed poly (vinyl alcohol)/chitosan-based films and combined them with the photoresponse properties of fluorescent carbon quantum dots extracted from natural ginger plants to develop a series of photodynamic infection wound dressings with high antibacterial properties (PCH-CDs). The composite film adapted to the patient's wound motion, had reliable mechanical properties. The results showed that the incorporation of carbon quantum dots enabled PCH-CDs2 to induce ROS production efficiently under light irradiation, and >2.20 Log10 CFU/mL of Staphylococcus aureus was inactivated after irradiation with a 405 nm light source for 40 min. The composite film showed excellent bacteriostatic and healing effects in a mouse model of infected wounds. Therefore, composite films provide efficient photodynamic antimicrobial therapy, which are promising candidate materials for the clinical application of infected wound dressings in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets generated during and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Skin is the organ most jeopardized by adverse external factors as a biological barrier between the human body and the external environment [1–3]. Wounds of varying degrees of severity can lead to pathological changes such as immune dysregulation and metabolic disorders, and can even lead to life-threatening shock [4,5]. The tightly regulated healing process of skin wounds consists of four distinct but interacting phases: hemostasis [6], inflammation [7], proliferation, and remodeling [8,9]. Bacterial infections frequently pose the greatest challenge to the healing process [10–12]. Therefore, wound dressings, as important pharmaceutical products for wound care, need to have a complete anti-infection and proliferation mechanism to avoid infection to a certain extent as well as to promote rapid wound healing.

Photodynamic therapy (PDT), the most promising alternative treatment modality for drug-resistant pathogens, has low invasiveness and broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties, does not require specific bacterial targets, and allows for repeatable, localized treatments to protect vital organs without easily triggering drug resistance [13–15]. Recently, much progress has been made in studies of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), CQDs are the member of carbon-based nanomaterials with unique physical and optical properties and are typically smaller than 10 nm in size. CQDs derived from natural extracts of herbs and plants have emerged as ideal sources of PSs, garnering attention due to their abundant availability and safety [16,17]. Curcumin, the main active ingredient derived from the perennial medicinal plant turmeric, is also a natural, inexpensive and readily available natural photosensitizer. In our previous study, we developed fluorescent turmeric carbon quantum dots (CDs) based on the extraction of natural photosensitizing constituents from turmeric using an economical and environmentally friendly synthesis method [18], which improved the poor water solubility and low utilization of curcumin while retaining the advantage of sustained and efficient bactericidal activity under light control as a natural photosensitizer [19]. CDs kill bacteria by absorbing light and interacting with oxygen to produce reactive oxygen species, with bacteria unlikely to develop resistance, which makes them ideal candidates for PDT wound dressing. The ever-changing and diverse forms of medical dressings at this stage include films, e-textiles, nanofibers, sponges, alginate, and other biomaterials. Among them, hydrogel films with high water content, porousness, and excellent stretchability are undoubtedly more suitable for use as dressings. Their thin and translucent appearance facilitates observation of the wound without removing the dressing, allows for gas and water penetration, keeps the wound moist, and mitigates the growth of microorganisms [20–23]. The excellent flexibility and tissue adhesion allow the film to mimic the properties of the skin, following the movement of the wound and reducing patient discomfort [24,25]. The film wound dressing loaded with turmeric CDs has the potential to overcome the defects of recurrent infections and low antimicrobial effects, meet the continuous needs of the wound healing process, and improve the therapeutic effect, and the key is how to construct an inexpensive and efficient antimicrobial film wound dressing with proper use of film matrix.

Chitosan (CS) [26–29] as a functional biopolymer, possesses antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties as well as good film-forming ability. Owing to its non-toxic and biodegradable properties, it is also a widely used natural polysaccharide antimicrobial substrate. Chitosan combines with negatively charged bacterial cell membrane molecules through its own positive charge, leading to leakage of proteins and other intracellular components, hindering the biosynthesis of the cell wall, thus affecting cellular metabolism to achieve the effect of growth inhibition and sterilization of microorganisms [30,31]. However, the presence of more hydrogen bonds between CS molecules leads to poor water solubility, so that the membrane prepared has poor mechanical properties and brittle [32]. Relevant scholars have chosen to improve the limitations of CS by cross-linking it with the flexible polymer poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA), which contains a large number of hydroxyl groups to exert its good mechanical properties, hydrophilicity and other advantages [33–35]. PVA is a biocompatible, biodegradable, and non-toxic polymer material that has almost no antimicrobial properties, but can play an important role in biomaterial engineering by physically cross-linking to form a hydrogel that incorporates antimicrobial agents. The hydrogen bonds formed between PVA and CS enhance the cohesion of the polymer network and endow the hydrogel with excellent mechanical properties [36–38]. Therefore, we envisioned that the clever cross-linking of CS and PVA could not only endow the film matrix with good mechanical properties to cover and protect the wounds, but also at the same time better control the release of turmeric CDs and improve drug stability and utilization efficiency.

By combining efficacy and manufacturing applications, a photomodulated highly effective antimicrobial film dressing (PCH-CDs) was developed for the treatment of chronically infected wounds (Fig. 1 ). The PCH-CDs were constructed by physically cross-linking PVA/CS and hyaluronic acid (HA), which is a highly hydrophilic naturally occurring polysaccharide that can promote the proliferative stage of wound repair by facilitating cell migration and mediating cellular signalling for wound healing [39]. Owing to the ingenious integration of many unique properties of the film components, PVA crosslinked CS film rapidly tissue adhesion behavior in the wound, CDs trigger efficient antimicrobial activity of PDT and inhibit inflammation after 405 nm light irradiation. PCH-CDs are expected to satisfy the stringent requirements for all stages of normal and infected athletic wound repair. Using Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus as model bacteria, we revealed the effects of the content of loaded photosensitive carbon dots and the phototherapy time on their in vitro antimicrobial properties, and explored the effectiveness of their in vivo wound healing promotion for bacterial infections. It is expected to inspire new vigor into the development of antimicrobial biomaterials and the clinical treatment of wounds.

Poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA, 98–99 % (mol/mol), MW: 115,000 Da), Chitosan (CS, MW: 50 KDa, deacetylation degree of 95 %), Hyaluronic acid (HA, MW: 40–100 kDa, deacetylation degree of 97 %) were bought from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The Turmeric was purchased from a local pharmacy (Nanning, China). Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were purchased from Shanghai Lu Wei Technology Co. All reagents used in the experiments were of analytical grade and high purity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CS and PCH-CDs films
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were prepared using a previously reported method [18]. Considering the safety and effective photodynamic antimicrobial effect, we screened and established the following preparation conditions. Briefly, turmeric powder was dispersed in ethanol, centrifuged after sonication, and filtered through a microporous membrane to obtain a preliminary extract solution of turmeric. Turmeric extract solution (20 mL) was carbonized at 180 °C for 12 h. The reaction solution was centrifuged, filtered through a 0.22 μM microporous membrane, and finally dried to obtain the brown powder CDs.

CS was first dissolved in a 1 % (v/v) aqueous acetic acid solution and stirred to form a clear solution. PVA was dissolved in water at a concentration of 6 % w/t at 90 °C for 2 h with magnetic stirring, while HA was dissolved in water. The 6 % w/t PVA, 1 % CS w/t and 0.5 % HA w/t solutions were mixed in ratios of 9:3:1, 6:3:1 and 3:3:1, respectively, and the total volume of the polymer solution was 20 mL by continuous stirring for 12 h to form a film-forming solution. The film-forming solution was centrifuged at 3000 r/min for 10 min to eliminate air bubbles and large particle precipitation, then transferred to a plastic mold (12 cm × 12 cm) and air-dried at 40 °C for 24 h to obtain a series of composite films PCH9, PCH6 and PCH3. The films were stored in a desiccator and were protected from light for use. PCH3 was selected as the representative composite hydrogel membrane matrix for subsequent experiments. 1 mL of CDs solution (0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mg/mL) was added to the PCH3 film-forming solution at a ratio of 1:20 and reacted for 24 h in the dark. Drug-loaded films with CDs concentrations of 0.25 wt%, 0.5 wt% and 1 wt% (relative to the total weight of the substrate) were prepared and defined as PCH-CDs1, PCH-CDs2 and PCH-CDs3, respectively.

The micromorphologies of the thin films were observed using a scanning electron microscope. After shredding, the thin film samples were mixed with solid potassium bromide to grind and press the tablets, and then scanned using an infrared spectrometer with a wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm−1. The transmittance of the thin film samples (3 cm × 1 cm) was measured using a ultraviolet-visible light (UV-VIS) spectrophotometer. A digital spiral micrometer was used to measure the film thickness. A contact angle measuring instrument (JC2000D, Shanghai Mid-Morning Digital Technology equipment) was used to measure the optical water contact angle of the film surface and capture the liquid deposition images. The film samples were cut into 25 mm × 115 mm rectangular strips, and the tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EAB) of the composite films with different ratios were measured using a universal testing machine (AGS-X, Shimadzu Company). The tensile speed was 5 mm/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Swelling ratio and water vapor permeability
ce:section.ce:para: The swelling ratio of the film was investigated by gravimetric analysis [40,41]. The 2 cm × 2 cm film sample (n = 3) was immersed in a phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.4) at 37 °C for a period of time. After the swelling equilibrium was reached, the samples were taken out and the water on the surface of the film was wiped off with filter paper, and the swelling ratio of the hydrogel films was calculated using the following formula: Swelling ratio (%) = (Ws − Wo) / Wo × 100 (where Ws and Wo correspond to the weights of swollen and initial dried hydrogel films, respectively).

The opening of the 50 mL centrifuge tube containing 20 g of completely dried silica gel was sealed with a thin film sample (4 cm × 4 cm) and placed in an airtight container containing saturated sodium chloride and maintained at 20 °C and 70 % relative humidity [42–44]. The centrifuge tube was weighed at a fixed time and the WVP of the film was calculated film.

WVP =
W × x
t × A × Δ P
where WVP was the water vapor permeability (g m−1 s−1 Pa−1), W was the weight gain of the centrifuge tube (g), x was the film thickness (m), t was time (s), A was the permeation area of the film sample (m2), and ΔP was the partial vapor pressure at 20 °C.

1,3-Diphenylbenzofuran (DPBF) was selected as a singlet oxygen scavenger to detect the ROS yield of thin films under 30 min of irradiation [45]. The sample treatment solution containing 2 mL of thin film and 30 μL of DPBF (5.0 × 10−5 mol/L) was added to the colorimetric plate and exposed to light source. In previous studies [18], CDs had the best fluorescence intensity at the excitation wavelengths of 400–440 wavelengths, and their bacterial inactivation effect was significantly improved when combined with a 405 nm light source (20 mW/cm2), so the 405 nm light source was chosen as the experimental light source. Furthermore, the semiconductor laser in the 405 nm wavelength band selected for the study have high power stability, continuously adjustable output power, and ease of operation, allowing for the generation of both high and low power outputs. This laser is suitable for applications such as fluorescence excitation, spectral analysis, and photodynamic irradiation, making it widely used in medical instruments. The sample solution was irradiated with a 405 nm laser for 3 min, after which the change of the absorption peak at 416 nm was measured using a UV spectrophotometer. This procedure was repeated until the cumulative irradiation time reached 30 min. Pure water was used as the control group, and the entire process was conducted in dark conditions to prevent interference from other light sources.

Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli) and Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus) were cultured in nutrient broth medium at 37 °C with shaking for 8 h, then washed twice with 0.9 % NaCl solution and diluted to OD600 = 0.5 to obtain the bacterial solution at concentration of 107 CFU/mL. First, 100 μL of film-forming solution was directly distributed in a 96-well plate to prepare the film, and 200 μL of bacterial solution was added to the 96-well plate. After incubation at 37 °C for 30 min, the bacterial solution in each well was irradiated with 405 nm laser light (0.5 W/cm2) for 10, 20, 30, and 40 min, respectively. Subsequently, the bacterial solution in each well plate was diluted with sterile 0.9 % NaCl, and 30 μL of the bacterial suspension was spread onto agar plates and cultured at 37 °C for 24 h. The antibacterial properties of the films were evaluated by quantitative colony counting [46,47]. Simultaneously, the bacterial suspensions from the different treatments were stained with 10 μg/mL of 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) and 50 μg/mL of propidium iodide (PI) for 30 min, then the fluorescent signals were observed using an inverted fluorescence microscope.

To evaluate the cytotoxicity of the composite film, hydrogel films (2 cm × 2 cm) were separately immersed in 3 mL of culture medium solution for 24 h at 37 °C and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, acting as the hydrogel film extraction solution for cell experiments. L929 cell suspension (200 μL) containing 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1640 nutrient solution was added to the 96-well plate, and different hydrogel film extraction solution were added for 24 h culture. The CCK-8 solution was added and co-cultivated for 2 h, and the optical density of the solution at 450 nm was measured using an enzyme labeling instrument.

The optical density (ODS) was measured with hydrogel film extraction solution as the experimental group, the optical density value (ODC) was measured with cell suspension and medium as the control group, and the optical density value (ODB) was measured with pure medium without cell suspension as the blank control group.

Cell scratch test: L929 cells at a concentration of 5 × 104/mL were inoculated on a 6-well culture plate and cultured at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 until the cells were fused into a monolayer. The cells were scratched with 100 μL sterile spear and washed twice with aseptic PBS buffer. Different film extracts (2 mL) were added into each well, and DMEM medium without samples was used as the control group. The scratched area was photographed and recorded every 12 h. The scratched areas were photographed and recorded. The cell migration was observed, and the cell migration rate was analyzed using an ImageJ software. Where S0 and St represent the scratch areas before and after the intervention, respectively [48,49].

Blood clotting test: the sample film was cut and laid in 48 plates for preheating at 37 °C. The 50 uL of citrated whole blood was added to the orifice plate and mixed evenly, and the pore plate was washed three times with physiological salt at a fixed time to stop coagulation and retain blood clots.

Treatment of infected wounds in mice: The animal use protocol listed below has been reviewed and approved by Guangxi University of Chinese Medicine Institutional Welfare and Ethical Committee (approval No. DW20240319016). After the female mice (body weight 25–30 g) were fed for 7 days, they were randomly divided into 4 groups. Each group underwent different treatments, including blank group (n = 7), streptomycin group (n = 7), PCH3 group + light irradiation (n = 7), PCH-CDs2 group + light irradiation (n = 7), where +Light means combined with irradiation for 30 min (405 nm, 20 mW/cm2). A round full-thickness skin wound of 1 cm was cut on the back of mice, and the wound was smeared with 100 uL of S. aureus bacterial solution (1.0 × 107 CFU/mL) to simulate the infected wound [50,51]. After 24 h, the infected wounds in the corresponding groups were treated with 200 μL of sterile saline, streptomycin solution and sample film. Among them, the group given radiation treatment needed to irradiate the wound for 30 min with light while avoiding interference from other light sources. The size of the wound was recorded on the 1st, 3rd, 6th and 9th day, and the bacteria on the wound surface were collected and quantified by plate counting. On the 11th day, the wound samples were collected and fixed with 10 % paraformaldehyde for pathological analysis. Histological evaluation with H&E, Masson's trichrome and immunofluorescence staining.

All experiments were repeated at least three times, and all data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). The results were statistically analyzed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). Statistical significance is denoted as follows: *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001.

In order to obtain film matrices with high biocompatibility, good mechanical properties, and synergistic tissue adhesion repair, we chose to cross-link chitosan with PVA and HA in different ratios to prepare a series of uniformly shaped, transparent composite films, PCH3, PCH6, and PCH9. We also loaded 0.25 wt%, 0.5 wt%, and 1.0 wt% concentrations of CDs to the films, respectively, with the expectation of improving the antimicrobial properties of the films, as well as its physical and mechanical properties. Under the SEM (Fig. 2a), the surface morphology of the dried films was smooth and crack-free, and it was observed that the incorporation of carbon quantum dots had no significant effect on the morphology of the films, and there was good homogeneous interphase fusion between the substrate and the carbon quantum. The CS and the PVA exerted different influences on the film-forming process of the composite films, and played a synergistic modulation on the structure [52]. As the PVA content increases, the cross-section becomes denser, while the PCH3 loose laminated structure can be more sufficient to expand the film's specific surface area and water contact area, providing drug inlet and outlet channels, and increasing the swelling rate of the film to ensure the dressing's ability to absorb and store water.

The cross-linking reaction was demonstrated using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2b). The spectrum of CS shows broad absorption peaks of hydroxyl and amino stretching vibrations of the chitosan ring near 3430 cm−1, and the absorption peak at 2925 cm−1 corresponds to the CH stretching vibration on the chitosan ring. Amide group bands were observed at 1648 cm−1 (stretching of CO vibration in amide I) and 1540 cm−1 (NH bending of amide II). A prominent band at 1407 cm−1 was due to C ̶ N stretching vibration, and the absorption peak observed at 1027 cm−1 was assigned to the asymmetric stretching vibration of C–O–C which was from the polysaccharide backbone [53–55]. The FTIR spectrum of PVA showed the OH stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group at 3450 cm−1, an absorption peak at 1638 cm−1 corresponding to the CO stretching of the acetate group, and a sharp peak at 1084 cm−1 associated with the CO stretching. The spectra of the composite films showed characteristic peaks of both polymers, and the peaks at 3450 cm−1 for the -OH and -NH2 stretching vibrations showed a tendency to slowly shift to the lower wavenumber region, indicating that intermolecular association of hydroxyl groups on the macromolecules occurred at a high probability. The intensity of the peak was found to increase at 1400 cm−1, probably due to the superposition of CH stretching by the increased proportion of PVA [56]. Compared with pure PVA and CS, no new peaks appeared in the FTIR spectra of the three PCH composite films, indicating that no covalent bonds were formed between PVA and CS, and that PVA was mixed with CS through intermolecular bonding interactions as well as hydrogen-bonded molecules [57,58]. The characteristic absorption peak of CS at 1027 cm−1 continued to weaken and almost disappeared as the ratio increased, probably because the proportion of CS decreased owing to the weaker absorption peak. IR spectroscopy analysis showed that there is an intermolecular interaction between CS and PVA, forming hydrogen bonds, which helps to improve the interfacial interaction between them and form a denser structure. The spectra of the PCH-CDs composite films were similar to the peak pattern of PCH, with no significant wavelength shifts. With an increase in the CDs content, the CDs absorption peak of the CH stretching vibration at 2941 cm−1 appeared in the spectral band of the film [18]. The broadband between 3500 and 3000 cm−1 showed a clear enhancement trend influenced by the abundant hydrogen bonding and NH bonding on the amino group. From the IR spectroscopy results, CS and PVA could be uniformly mixed to form cross-links for use as wound dressings. In addition, the turmeric carbon dots were successfully incorporated into the matrix.

Under macroscopic observation, the surfaces of the films were flat and smooth, with no graininess (Fig. 3a). The CS, PCH3, and PCH-CDs2 films generally exhibited a smooth and uniform, transparent appearance corresponding to their high visible transmittance (400–800 nm) in the UV–visible spectrum. In addition, the films prepared with different proportions of components showed a significant difference in the UV region (200–400 nm), with the PCH3 composite film exhibiting lower UV transmission (Fig. 3b). This indicated that with the addition of CDs, the colorless films turned yellow-brown, which enhanced the blocking ability of the composite films to UV light and caused a decrease in visible light transmission.

Some studies have shown that films with EAB higher than 70 % in the range of 2.5–16 N/mm2 (MPa) for TS are better able to resist the natural deformation of human skin [59]. The breaking stresses of PCH3, PCH6 and PCH9 film dressings were 6.45 ± 0.44 N, 9.08 ± 1.08 N, 10.04 ± 1.23 N. They reached a maximum stress of 8.57 ± 1.16 N/m2,11.04 ± 1.30 N/m2, 11.45 ± 1.19 N/m2, respectively, and fractured directly with an elongation in the range of 160–140 % (Fig. 3d). When the ratio of PVA:CS content in the composite film is 3:3 (PCH3) its elongation at break can reach 140 %. With an increase in PVA content, the film thickness increases with increased softness and reduced brittleness, and the elongation at break was elevated to 152 %. The destructive stress of PCH-CDs2 was 6.88 ± 0.45 N and the maximum stress was 8.97 ± 0.93 N/m2. The film loaded with CDs maintains the rigidity and toughness of the substrate to a certain extent. The films could be folded and curled at will, facilitating portability and storage. By applying the film to the human wrist to simulate the application of a sports wound, the film remained attached without falling off, showing good tissue adhesion (Fig. 3e). These properties ensure the integrity of the film during therapeutic use, and establish a foundation for achieving efficacy.

For wound dressings, good swelling properties mean that the material can rapidly absorb wound exudate to avoid wound maceration and microbial growth, while storing large amounts of water to maintain moderate wetness in dry wounds. The swelling rates of the composite films are shown in Fig. 4a. All the films were rapidly swollen in PBS buffer with a swelling rate of 189 %. In addition, films with low PVA and high CS contents exhibited higher water absorption and swelling rates. The CS molecule contains a large amount of -OH and -NH2, which can form hydrogen bonds with water molecules to bind with them. At the same time, with the increase in PVA content in the membrane, the spatial distance between the polymer molecules decreases and the molecular interaction force increases, forming a denser membrane structure, thus, the ability of water absorption and swelling decreases.

Often, too low or too high water vapor permeability may lead to excessive accumulation or excessive drying of wound exudate, therefore, wound dressings also need to maintain a moderate moisture content in the wound environment. As shown in Fig. 4b, the water vapor permeability of the composite films decreased as the ratio increased. As mentioned above, the increase in the PVA content in the film leads to enhanced intermolecular crosslinking and a denser film structure, which affects the diffusion of water vapor in the film matrix. Compared with PCH3, the water vapor permeability of PCH-CDs2 is also reduced, which can be explained by the addition of CDs making the structure of the membrane more compact and orderly, increasing the thickness of the membrane. Thus, it is more difficult for water vapor to pass through the pores of the membrane and slows down its diffusion.

The Fig. 4c shows the hydrostatic contact angle of the film. The contact angle reflects the water resistance of the film surface, and its magnitude is influenced by two main factors: one is the hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of the substance itself. Films with larger θ values exhibited higher hydrophobicity. The other is the surface properties of the solid material [60,61]. For hydrophilic surfaces, the larger the surface roughness, the smaller the contact angle. Since chitosan is rich in hydrophilic groups and is soluble in weakly acidic aqueous solutions, one might expect membranes prepared from chitosan solutions to be hydrophilic. However, studies have generally shown that chitosan films are hydrophobic, with explanations including the trapping of hydrophobic monomers during the drying of solid chitosan and the lack of hydrogen bonding groups on the surface of the chitosan film [62–64]. Therefore, films with high CS content have a water contact angle of >90°. The hydroxyl group within the PVA molecule forms a strong hydrogen bond with the water molecules, which leads to easy wetting of the film surface. The addition of PVA increased the hydrophilicity of the composite film surface, thereby reducing the water contact angle of the material. The contact angle of the PCH-CDs2 film was also slightly reduced. This can be explained that CDs may lead to weaker interaction forces between the PVA chains, which in turn prompted the formation of hydrogen bonds between the PVA chain segments and water molecules, enhanced the hydrophilicity of the composite film surface, and led to a smaller water contact angle of the material.

The cytotoxicity of composite films with different concentrations of turmeric CDs was evaluated by culturing mouse fibroblasts (L929) on films and the cell viability was measured after co-incubation with the films for 24 h. Fig. 5a shows that all the treated L929 cells showed high cell viability, indicating that the films had no potential toxicity. The cytotoxicity of the drug-laden composite films was largely dependent on the loaded drug content, and the CDs improved the toxicity of turmeric without affecting the cytotoxicity of the matrix. Thus, the drug-loaded composite films exhibited no significant toxic adverse effects and showed potential as transdermal drug delivery dressings.

The effect of blank and drug-laden membranes on L929 cell migration was evaluated by a scratch assay over 60 h. Compared to the control group, the PCH-CDs2 treated group migrated better, reaching a closure rate of >60 % within 24 h. The addition of turmeric carbon dots accelerated the process of scratch repair, and the corresponding curves established from the quantitative analysis showed that the cell closure rate was higher in the membrane containing 0.5 % CDs, which could reach 80 % closure rate at 40 h. This indicates that PCH-CDs2 facilitates cell migration and promotes fibroblast migration to the wound site during the healing process. Fibroblasts can not only form granulation tissue, but also synthesize and secrete collagen and migrate to the extracellular matrix, providing the possibility of therapeutic wound healing.

The dynamic whole blood coagulation test in vitro was used to evaluate the procoagulant ability of composite films to blood in vitro [65]. At room temperature, the whole blood clot was visible to the naked eye in 4 min in the control group, which was similar to the whole blood clotting time of 4–6 min in human. In contrast, the PCH-CDs2 film group quickly absorbed blood to form a clot and showed a deep red color with the extension of time, showing a rapid clotting effect within 2 min. Owing to the activation and aggregation of platelets by chitosan with high positive charge, chitosan film dressing plays an excellent role in reducing wound exudation and accelerating hemostasis.

Photosensitizer, excitation light, and molecular oxygen are the three major elements responsible for achieving photodynamic antibacterial activity. To verify the photosensitization mechanism and antibacterial effect of the film, we further investigated its ability to generate reactive oxygen species during the photodynamic process. Using 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) as a singlet oxygen trapping agent, the characteristic summit at 420 nm decreases rapidly when it is oxidized with singlet oxygen, and the quantum yield of singlet oxygen is calculated based on the change in absorbance (Fig. 6a-d). The solution containing CDs can efficiently produce reactive oxygen species under 405 nm irradiation for 30 min, and the fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly. After irradiation for only 1 min, the fluorescence intensity of CDs solution at 420 nm decreased by 20 times as much as that of the blank group, but there was almost no fluorescence change in the blank group without the photosensitizer. With an increase in irradiation time, PCH-CDs2 can still induce the fluorescence intensity of DPBF solution to decrease gradually, and the quantum yield of singlet oxygen is slightly lower but more uniform than that of CDs, indicating that loading in the film matrix may delay the light trapping ability of CDs, but PCH-CDS2 can still effectively stimulate the production of a large number of reactive oxygen species under light, and achieve efficient photodynamic therapy.

Oxygen damage to biomolecules (proteins, lipids, nucleic acids) can be caused by reactive oxygen species, which further achieves the purpose of killing pathogenic microorganisms. Based on this, a test of the actual photodynamic antimicrobial effect of the films was carried out using the co-culture colony counting method. Fig. 7b shows the results of co-culture of composite films with different CDs contents with Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, respectively, and the counting of bacterial colonies was performed to obtain Fig. 7c-d. Without the dressing treatment, the bacterial content decreased only slightly after up to 30 min of light exposure, with a negligible inhibitory effect of UV irradiation. Benefiting from the antibacterial flair of chitosan and the dark toxicity of CDs, the films without CDs, also showed a certain attenuation of bacterial proliferation compared to the control group. Under the intervention of 405 nm light, the antibacterial properties of the films loaded with CDs were significantly enhanced, and the inhibitory effect showed a dependence on light exposure time, and S. aureus exceeding 2.20 Log10 CFU/mL was inactivated by the PCH-CDs2 films after 40 min of irradiation. Interestingly, the inhibitory effect of PCH-CDs2 was superior to that of PCH-CDs3 under more prolonged light exposure. A similar situation was found and explained by the fact that the clustering of excess CDs, may cause a diminished inhibitory effect caused by a decrease in the density of surface-active dots. Therefore, controlling the infectious condition requires controlling the drug dosage window without blindly increasing the dosage. Similarly, the total number of inactivated colonies of E. coli after co-culture with PCH-CDs1, PCH-CDs2, and PCH-CDs3 films reached 1.28, 1.36, and 1.45 Log10 CFU/mL, respectively. Thus, both the CDs as the photosensitive materials and the irradiation duration are the key factors affecting the antimicrobial effect of the films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of live and dead bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: At the same time, the results of the live and dead cell staining tests were almost consistent with the above-mentioned antibacterial activity [66,67]. DAPI and PI co-staining were used to distinguish between living and dead bacteria (Fig. 7e). The results of the fluorescence staining showed that the expression of red fluorescence in the control group was significantly lower than that in the other groups, and the bacteria remained alive. Different intensities of red fluorescence appeared in the images of treatment of PCH3 + 405 nm irradiation for 30 min and treatment of PCH-CDs2 without light, with a few bacteria showing death due to the disruption of cell membranes, owing to the bacterial inhibitory effect of chitosan in the films and the dark toxicity of CDs. In sharp contrast, treatment of PCH-CDs2 + 405 nm irradiation for 30 min showed the highest number of dead cells (large-area red fluorescence), indicating the strongest bactericidal effect. All these results showed that the PCH3-CDs-mediated PDT combination significantly enhanced the antibacterial activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo wound healing in infected full-thickness skin defect model
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above experimental results, PCH-CDs film can be used as a potential bacteriostatic wound dressing for the treatment of infected wounds. Among them, PCH3, PCH-CDs2 with the best antibacterial effect were selected as the representatives of the experimental group. A full-thickness skin wound model of mice infected with S. aureus was used to simulate the infected wound environment and evaluate the antibacterial ability and wound healing behavior of the membrane in vivo (Fig. 8a). Streptomycin was used as the positive control group. According to the wound size image (Fig. 8b), the wound area was calculated for 11 days, and the wound area in each group shrank to a certain extent. On the third day, after treatment with PCH-CDs2, the wound area was 69.22 ± 2.81 %, significantly lower than that of the control group (89.20 ± 3.24 %) and the positive control group (81.04 ± 2.39 %). On the ninth day of administration, the wound area of the mice was significantly reduced, and the scab began to harden. After 11 days of administration, the wounds of mice in the PCH-CDs2 group healed almost completely, and the wounds were covered with new epidermis and surrounded by hair growth. The wounds in the two control groups were still healing, with wound area of 15.15 ± 2.49 % and 15.20 ± 2.99 %, respectively. At the same time, bacteria were taken from the skin surface of the wound and cultured on medium, Streptomycin as an aminoglycoside antibiotic showed the strongest antimicrobial effect in the early stage of treatment. With the prolongation of the treatment time, the antimicrobial effect of PCH-CDs2 increased, and the colony-forming units on the surface of the medium of the PCH-CDs2 group were significantly smaller than that of the control groups by the ninth day, which was a significant difference (Fig. 8f). In conclusion, PCH-CDs2 hydrogel showed desirable antimicrobial activity during wound healing, preventing bacterial colonization and promoting wound healing.

Noticeably, the main mechanisms of the wound healing process in humans are re-epithelialization and granulation tissue formation, whereas the most important limitation of the rodent model is that epithelial regeneration accounts for approximately 40 % of the healing of total excision wounds, which are more dependent on their wound contraction effects [68,69]. Powerful contraction leads to faster wound closure, a healing process that is not entirely similar to the body's normal skin repair mechanisms, and the composite hydrogel film may be suitable for patients with severe infections or those who need to undergo long-term restorative treatments.

At the same time, changes in body weight of mice in each group were recorded for 11 days (Fig. 8g). After modeling, the body weight of mice did not decrease significantly, the production of wounds and light treatment did not affect the appetites of mice, and there was no significant difference in the change of body weight of mice in each group. It shows that the administration of PCH-CDs2 dressing combined with PDT is a safe and effective treatment.

H&E staining can track the formation of fibroblasts and hair follicles in mouse skin, and collagen deposition is the main component of the wound extracellular matrix in the proliferation stage, which can enhance the strength of newborn skin [70]. The skin of the wound on the 11th day was stained with Hype and Masson staining to study the histological changes in the wound during the wound repair process.

As shown in Fig. 9a-d, on day 11, epidermal necrosis was absent in the skin tissue of the Control group, and a large area of degenerative necrosis of collagen fibers in the dermis was still observed (orange arrow), with loss of the collagen fiber structure. The necrosis of the dermis involved the subcutaneous muscle layer (yellow arrows), and localized areas of myofibrillar degeneration and necrosis with massive inflammatory cell infiltration (blue-green arrows) were observed, with some hyperplastic collagen fibers. Localized hemorrhages with scattered erythrocytes (light blue arrows) were observed. The PCH3 group also showed localized areas of degenerative necrosis in the subcutaneous fat and muscle layers with varying degrees of inflammatory cell infiltration and one case of Munros microabscess. The wound was still in the inflammatory phase and the healing process was delayed. In contrast, the epidermal structure of the skin tissue in the positive drug group was distinctly keratinized. Many collagen fibers in the dermis were degenerated and necrotic, and necrosis was observed in the subcutaneous fat layer. Some muscle fibers were degenerated and necrotic, and there was a small amount of neutrophil and lymphocyte inflammatory infiltration. The epidermal structure of the skin tissue in the PCH-CDs2 group was more complete and clearer, with scab formation in local areas and mild thickening of the epidermal layer (green arrow). A small amount of inflammatory cell infiltration was observed; at the same time, dense accumulation of mature collagen fibers was observed, as well as the formation of new capillaries (black and blue arrows), and the wound healing entered the proliferative remodeling stage. In summary, compared with the control group, the extent of lesions in the blank film group was not significantly different, and the extent of lesions in the PCH-CDs2 groups was relatively mild, effectively promoting the transformation of the inflammatory phase to the proliferative phase, improving collagen deposition, and facilitating wound healing.

CD31 is a common index for evaluating vascular endothelial cells during regeneration, and the statistical results (Fig. 9f) showed that the number of CD31 microvessels in the skin tissue of mice in the PCH-CDs2 group was significantly higher than that in the control group (P < 0.01). At this time, the wound was in the proliferative phase, and the new blood vessels provided oxygen and nutrients to meet the metabolic requirements for re-epithelialization and granulation of proliferating cells. In the inflammatory phase of the wound, proinflammatory cytokines activate the complement system, leading to an increase in inflammation. The inflammatory response in the new tissue was verified by examining TNF-α factor expression (Fig. 9g). Both the control and PCH3 groups showed high expression of TNF-α, indicating that they had poor antibacterial and anti-inflammatory capacities against trauma. In contrast, the PCH-CDs2 group exhibited the lowest TNF-α expression (p < 0.01), followed by the erythromycin group, both of which effectively reduced the inflammatory response and exerted positive anti-inflammatory effects during the wound recovery period.

Topical dermal administration might also induce unexpected toxic reactions by accumulating in different organs through the blood circulatory system. Therefore, biocompatibility is also a key requirement for wound healing dressing materials in clinical applications [21,46,71]. Therefore, mice were sacrificed after 11 days of administration, and H&E staining was performed on five substantial organs of each group to evaluate the in vivo compatibility (Fig. 9h). It could be seen that there was no significant difference in organ indices among the groups, no significant damage to cardiomyocytes, uniform distribution of hepatocytes, no significant heterogeneous changes in alveoli, and no lesions appeared in the cells of kidney sections. This indicates that the positive drug and membrane treatment showed no signs of toxicity to the organs of mice, which is a safe method of drug delivery that can be used for wound healing treatment.

It is worth mentioning that the light source selected for the composite membrane in this study belongs to blue-violet light, which has limitations in its depth of tissue penetration, and therefore is applicable only to a small animal model. Need for a short wavelength and long irradiation time are limitations. In our future studies, we will try our best to investigate the systems that can utilize longer wavelengths with greater penetration capabilities.

In this study, by using polymer PVA and CS as film-forming substrates, a safe and degradable composite film dressing was prepared without a chemical cross-linking agent, which has good mechanical properties, is close to human skin and can meet the wound movement of patients (Figs. 3–4). The excellent biocompatibility and migration closure capabilities of the composite film enabled it to achieve a therapeutic effect of accelerated wound healing (Fig. 5). Owing to the high ROS yield of CDs under blue light irradiation, PCH-CD exhibited highly efficient bacterial inhibition of both E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. 7). It is worth mentioning that the combined efficacy of the composite film for the inhibition and repair of infected wounds exceeded that of streptomycin in the in vivo application experiments in mice, proving its antibiotic therapeutic substitution possibility (Fig. 8–9). This study provides a new strategy for the design and preparation of materials with excellent properties for skin healing. In addition, the carbon quantum dot composite film uses the short wavelength light, which has a limited ability to penetrate the tissue, thus it is more suitable for antimicrobial treatment of the superficial skin, and it is expected to carry out the treatment of deeper tissues at long wavelengths in the future.
